words.exampleNL: Ik ga om acht uur naar mijn werk.
words.exampleEN: I go to work at eight o'clock.
words.audioHint: The 'w' sounds like English 'v'. The 'e' sounds like 'e' in 'bed'.

words.exampleNL: Ik werk vijf dagen per week.
words.exampleEN: I work five days per week.
words.audioHint: The 'w' sounds like 'v'. The 'e' sounds like 'e' in 'bed'.

words.exampleNL: Ik zoek een nieuwe baan.
words.exampleEN: I'm looking for a new job.
words.audioHint: The 'aa' is a long 'ah' sound.

words.exampleNL: Wat is uw beroep?
words.exampleEN: What is your profession?
words.audioHint: The 'oe' sounds like 'oo'. Stress on the second syllable.

words.exampleNL: Ons kantoor is in het centrum.
words.exampleEN: Our office is in the city center.
words.audioHint: The 'oo' sounds like 'oh'. Stress on the second syllable.

words.exampleNL: Mijn collega's zijn heel aardig.
words.exampleEN: My colleagues are very nice.
words.audioHint: The 'g' is guttural. Stress on the second syllable.

words.exampleNL: Mijn baas is heel tevreden over mijn werk.
words.exampleEN: My boss is very satisfied with my work.
words.audioHint: The 'aa' is a long 'ah'. Informal word for boss.

words.exampleNL: De manager heeft een vergadering.
words.exampleEN: The manager has a meeting.
words.audioHint: Borrowed from English. Pronunciation is close to English but with Dutch intonation.

words.exampleNL: Het salaris wordt maandelijks betaald.
words.exampleEN: The salary is paid monthly.
words.audioHint: Stress on the second syllable. The 'a' sounds like 'ah'.

words.exampleNL: Het minimumloon stijgt volgend jaar.
words.exampleEN: The minimum wage is rising next year.
words.audioHint: The 'oo' sounds like 'oh'. More informal than 'salaris'.

words.exampleNL: Ik wil solliciteren bij die firma.
words.exampleEN: I want to apply at that company.
words.audioHint: Stress on the fourth syllable. The 'ee' sounds like 'ay'.

words.exampleNL: Ik heb een sollicitatie verstuurd.
words.exampleEN: I sent a job application.
words.audioHint: The 'tie' ending is pronounced 'tsee'. Long word typical of formal Dutch.

words.exampleNL: Ik heb morgen een sollicitatiegesprek.
words.exampleEN: I have a job interview tomorrow.
words.audioHint: Very long compound word. 'Gesprek' means conversation. The 'g' is guttural.

words.exampleNL: Vergeet niet je cv mee te sturen.
words.exampleEN: Don't forget to send your CV.
words.audioHint: Pronounced as separate letters: 'say-vay'. Short for 'curriculum vitae'.

words.exampleNL: Schrijf een goede motivatiebrief.
words.exampleEN: Write a good cover letter.
words.audioHint: Compound of 'motivatie' + 'brief' (letter). Common requirement for Dutch job applications.

words.exampleNL: Ik heb een contract voor een jaar.
words.exampleEN: I have a one-year contract.
words.audioHint: Similar to English. Stress on the second syllable.

words.exampleNL: Ik werk fulltime, veertig uur per week.
words.exampleEN: I work full-time, forty hours per week.
words.audioHint: Borrowed from English. Pronunciation is close to English.

words.exampleNL: Veel Nederlanders werken parttime.
words.exampleEN: Many Dutch people work part-time.
words.audioHint: The Netherlands has one of the highest rates of part-time work in Europe.

words.exampleNL: De vergadering begint om tien uur.
words.exampleEN: The meeting starts at ten o'clock.
words.audioHint: The 'v' sounds like 'f'. The 'g' is guttural. Stress on the second syllable.

words.exampleNL: Wanneer ga je op vakantie?
words.exampleEN: When are you going on vacation?
words.audioHint: The 'v' is soft. The 'tie' is pronounced 'tsee'. Dutch workers get generous vacation days.

words.exampleNL: Ik heb nog vijf vakantiedagen over.
words.exampleEN: I still have five vacation days left.
words.audioHint: Compound of 'vakantie' + 'dag'. Most Dutch workers get at least 20 vacation days per year.

words.exampleNL: Mijn werkgever betaalt de ziekteverzekering.
words.exampleEN: My employer pays the health insurance.
words.audioHint: Compound of 'werk' (work) + 'gever' (giver). Both 'g' sounds are guttural.

words.exampleNL: Het bedrijf heeft honderd werknemers.
words.exampleEN: The company has one hundred employees.
words.audioHint: Compound of 'werk' (work) + 'nemer' (taker). The 'ee' sounds like 'ay'.

words.exampleNL: Het bedrijf zit in Amsterdam.
words.exampleEN: The company is located in Amsterdam.
words.audioHint: The 'ij' sounds like 'eye'. The 'f' at the end is voiced.

words.exampleNL: Heb je ervaring met programmeren?
words.exampleEN: Do you have experience with programming?
words.audioHint: The 'v' sounds like 'f'. Stress on the second syllable.

words.exampleNL: Welke opleiding heb je gedaan?
words.exampleEN: What education did you do?
words.audioHint: The 'ei' sounds like 'eye'. Important for CVs and job applications.

words.exampleNL: Het bedrijf heeft tien mensen ontslagen.
words.exampleEN: The company fired ten people.
words.audioHint: The 'aa' is a long 'ah'. Past participle is 'ontslagen'.

words.exampleNL: Ik ga ontslag nemen bij mijn werk.
words.exampleEN: I'm going to resign from my job.
words.audioHint: The 'g' in 'ontslag' is guttural. 'Ontslag' means dismissal.

words.exampleNL: Ik loop stage bij een ziekenhuis.
words.exampleEN: I'm doing an internship at a hospital.
words.audioHint: Borrowed from French. The 'g' sounds like 'zh' as in 'measure', not guttural here.

words.exampleNL: De leraar geeft les in wiskunde.
words.exampleEN: The teacher teaches mathematics.
words.audioHint: The 'ee' sounds like 'ay'. The 'aa' is a long 'ah'.

words.exampleNL: De lerares is heel geduldig.
words.exampleEN: The teacher is very patient.
words.audioHint: Feminine form of 'leraar'. The '-es' ending marks the feminine.

words.exampleNL: Ik heb een advocaat nodig.
words.exampleEN: I need a lawyer.
words.audioHint: Stress on the last syllable. Also means 'avocado' in some contexts!

words.exampleNL: Zij is ingenieur bij Philips.
words.exampleEN: She is an engineer at Philips.
words.audioHint: French-origin word. The 'g' here sounds like 'zh', not guttural. The 'eu' is a unique Dutch sound.

words.exampleNL: Onze accountant doet de belastingaangifte.
words.exampleEN: Our accountant does the tax return.
words.audioHint: Similar to English. The 'ou' sounds like 'ow'.

words.exampleNL: De programmeur bouwt een nieuwe website.
words.exampleEN: The programmer is building a new website.
words.audioHint: The 'g' is guttural. The 'eu' is a unique Dutch sound - round lips for 'oo' but say 'uh'.

words.exampleNL: De kok maakt een heerlijk gerecht.
words.exampleEN: The chef is making a delicious dish.
words.audioHint: Short and simple. The 'o' sounds like 'o' in 'hot'.

words.exampleNL: De politieagent helpt de toerist.
words.exampleEN: The police officer helps the tourist.
words.audioHint: Compound of 'politie' + 'agent'. The 'g' in 'agent' is guttural.

words.exampleNL: De brandweermannen blussen het vuur.
words.exampleEN: The firefighters extinguish the fire.
words.audioHint: Compound of 'brandweer' (fire department) + 'man'. The 'w' sounds like 'v'.

words.exampleNL: Ik werk de hele dag achter de computer.
words.exampleEN: I work behind the computer all day.
words.audioHint: Similar to English but with Dutch pronunciation. The 'u' sounds like 'uu'.

words.exampleNL: Ik stuur je een e-mail.
words.exampleEN: I'll send you an email.
words.audioHint: Similar to English. Written with a hyphen in Dutch.

words.exampleNL: De deadline is volgende week vrijdag.
words.exampleEN: The deadline is next Friday.
words.audioHint: Borrowed from English. Same pronunciation.

words.exampleNL: We werken aan een nieuw project.
words.exampleEN: We're working on a new project.
words.audioHint: The 'j' sounds like 'y'. Stress on the second syllable.

words.exampleNL: Ik neem een pauze van een half uur.
words.exampleEN: I'm taking a half-hour break.
words.audioHint: The 'au' sounds like 'ow' in 'cow'. The final 'e' is a schwa.

words.exampleNL: De lunchpauze is van twaalf tot een.
words.exampleEN: The lunch break is from twelve to one.
words.audioHint: Compound of 'lunch' + 'pauze'. The 'au' sounds like 'ow'.

words.exampleNL: Ik moet vanavond overwerken.
words.exampleEN: I have to work overtime tonight.
words.audioHint: Separable verb: 'over' splits off. The 'w' sounds like 'v'.

words.exampleNL: Ik werk twee dagen per week thuis.
words.exampleEN: I work from home two days a week.
words.audioHint: The 'ui' in 'thuis' sounds like 'ow' with rounded lips. Very common in the Netherlands.

words.exampleNL: Ik werk als zzp'er.
words.exampleEN: I work as a freelancer.
words.audioHint: Abbreviation for 'zelfstandige zonder personeel'. Very common in the Netherlands.

words.exampleNL: Je moet belasting betalen over je inkomen.
words.exampleEN: You have to pay tax on your income.
words.audioHint: The 'a' sounds like 'ah'. Important word for working in the Netherlands.

words.exampleNL: De vakbond onderhandelt over het salaris.
words.exampleEN: The union negotiates about the salary.
words.audioHint: Compound of 'vak' (trade) + 'bond' (union). The 'd' at the end sounds like 't'.

words.exampleNL: Mijn vader gaat volgend jaar met pensioen.
words.exampleEN: My father is retiring next year.
words.audioHint: The 'oe' sounds like 'oo'. 'Met pensioen gaan' means to retire.

words.exampleNL: Ik heb een loonsverhoging gekregen.
words.exampleEN: I got a pay raise.
words.audioHint: Long compound word. 'Loon' (wage) + 'verhoging' (increase). The 'g' sounds are guttural.

words.exampleNL: Mijn werkplek is bij het raam.
words.exampleEN: My workplace is by the window.
words.audioHint: Compound of 'werk' (work) + 'plek' (place). The 'w' sounds like 'v'.

words.exampleNL: De vergaderruimte is bezet.
words.exampleEN: The meeting room is occupied.
words.audioHint: Compound of 'vergader' (meeting) + 'ruimte' (space). The 'ui' sounds like 'ow' with rounded lips.

words.exampleNL: Ik heb een grote klus af.
words.exampleEN: I finished a big task.
words.audioHint: The 'u' sounds like 'u' in 'put'. Informal word for a task or job.

words.exampleNL: Zij heeft promotie gekregen!
words.exampleEN: She got a promotion!
words.audioHint: The 'tie' ending is pronounced 'tsee'. Stress on the second syllable.

words.exampleNL: De winkelier opent de winkel om negen uur.
words.exampleEN: The shopkeeper opens the shop at nine o'clock.
words.audioHint: The 'w' sounds like 'v'. The 'ie' sounds like 'ee'. Stress on the last syllable.

words.exampleNL: De boer heeft koeien en schapen.
words.exampleEN: The farmer has cows and sheep.
words.audioHint: The 'oe' sounds like 'oo' in 'poor'. Farming is a significant part of Dutch culture.

words.exampleNL: We moeten de loodgieter bellen.
words.exampleEN: We need to call the plumber.
words.audioHint: Compound of 'lood' (lead) + 'gieter' (caster). The 'g' is guttural. The 'ie' sounds like 'ee'.

words.exampleNL: De chauffeur van de bus is heel vriendelijk.
words.exampleEN: The bus driver is very friendly.
words.audioHint: French origin. The 'ch' sounds like 'sh'. The 'eu' is a unique Dutch sound.

words.exampleNL: Hij is al drie maanden werkloos.
words.exampleEN: He has been unemployed for three months.
words.audioHint: Compound of 'werk' (work) + '-loos' (without). The 'oo' sounds like 'oh'.

words.exampleNL: Ik heb me ingeschreven bij een uitzendbureau.
words.exampleEN: I registered at a temp agency.
words.audioHint: Long compound word. 'Uitzend' (send out) + 'bureau' (office). Common way to find work in NL.

words.exampleNL: De opdrachtgever is tevreden met het resultaat.
words.exampleEN: The client is satisfied with the result.
words.audioHint: Compound of 'opdracht' (assignment) + 'gever' (giver). The 'g' sounds are guttural.

words.exampleNL: Zij is een succesvolle ondernemer.
words.exampleEN: She is a successful entrepreneur.
words.audioHint: The 'ee' in 'nemer' sounds like 'ay'. Derived from 'ondernemen' (to undertake).

words.exampleNL: Veel studenten hebben een bijbaan.
words.exampleEN: Many students have a side job.
words.audioHint: Compound of 'bij' (side/extra) + 'baan' (job). The 'ij' sounds like 'eye'.

words.exampleNL: Het minimumloon is dit jaar gestegen.
words.exampleEN: The minimum wage has risen this year.
words.audioHint: Compound of 'minimum' + 'loon' (wage). The 'oo' in 'loon' sounds like 'oh'.
